food_subgroup: Chinese cold dishes / starch-based jelly preparations

ontology.foodon_label: noodle food product (broad class used as closest defensible anchor for starch-sheet preparations)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Chinese starch jelly sheets (Da La Pi). FOODON_03315671 (noodle food product) is used as the closest defensible structural analog. A dedicated term does not currently exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Starch-based noodles and pasta — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for mung bean or potato starch jelly sheets
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Chinese starch jelly sheets. A00LN (starch-based noodles) is the closest defensible category. Documented as closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Chinese cold dish preparation. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: potato starch or mung bean starch sheets

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Sichuan Spicy Jelly Sheets is approximately 250 g, representing one individual plate as commonly served in Chinese restaurants or home settings.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mung Bean Starch Noodles (closest approved analog) combined with component estimation for sesame paste dressing and vegetable garnishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sichuan Spicy Jelly Sheets (Da La Pi) exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for mung bean starch noodles (FDC ID 168869), sesame paste (FDC ID 169560), chili oil, and raw cucumber/carrot, weighted by typical recipe proportions. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this component-based estimation and have been retained with source upgraded to USDA-anchored estimation. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.2 g/100 g based on sesame paste fatty acid profile from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mung bean starch noodles and resistant starch effect of cooled starch gels — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mung bean vermicelli and cooled starch preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Da La Pi (Sichuan Spicy Jelly Sheets). GI of 45 (low) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean vermicelli (GI ~26–39) and potato starch noodles (GI ~50–56), averaged and adjusted upward slightly for the mixed starch composition and dressing fat content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 33.75) / 100 ≈ 15.2 g. The fat content from sesame paste further moderates postprandial glucose response. This is a defensible analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg/100 g; ~1125 mg per 250 g serving) from soy sauce and chili oil dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce in the dressing, as a standard 250 g serving provides approximately 1125 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) due to resistant starch formation in cooled mung bean or potato starch sheets, moderated further by sesame paste fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of approximately 45 and moderate glycemic load of ~15 per serving make this dish a relatively suitable carbohydrate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided portion size is controlled and the high sodium content from the dressing is accounted for in the overall dietary plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (450 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (7.5 g/100 g) from sesame paste and chili oil, partially offset by favorable unsaturated fatty acid profile of sesame
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content is the primary cardiovascular concern; however, the fat in this dish is predominantly unsaturated (from sesame paste), which is not inherently atherogenic — individuals with cardiovascular disease should focus on sodium reduction and portion control rather than fat avoidance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sesame paste provides antioxidants (vitamin E, sesamin); garlic and chili have potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish may be tolerated well by most individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, though those sensitive to nightshades or spicy foods should monitor their response to chili oil and Sichuan peppercorns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sichuan Spicy Jelly Sheets, known in Chinese as 大拉皮 (Dà lā pí), is a popular Chinese cold dish originating from Northeastern China (Dongbei) that has been widely adopted with a Sichuan-style spicy twist. The dish is built around wide, thick, transparent sheets made from potato starch or mung bean starch, which are boiled and then cooled to develop a uniquely chewy, elastic, and slightly slippery texture. The cooling process promotes resistant starch formation, which lowers the glycemic index to approximately 45 (low GI). The sheets are tossed in a complex, layered dressing combining creamy sesame paste (tahini), chili oil, Sichuan peppercorns (which impart a characteristic numbing sensation known as 'má'), soy sauce, garlic, and vinegar. The dish is typically garnished with julienned cucumber and carrot for freshness and crunch, and sometimes wood ear mushrooms or shredded egg omelet. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 125 kcal, 2.2 g protein, 7.5 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from sesame), and 13.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at 450 mg/100 g due to the soy-sauce-based dressing, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and vegan-friendly (when omelet is omitted), halal, and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a cold appetizer or side dish during lunch, dinner, or as a snack, particularly in summer. Clinically, the low GI and unsaturated fat profile are favorable for glycemic management, but sodium content requires portion control for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sichuan Spicy Jelly Sheets (Da La Pi) exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analogs (mung bean starch noodles, sesame paste, chili oil, raw vegetables) weighted by typical recipe proportions. The input AI-generated values are broadly consistent with this estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE: No exact approved-source entry exists for Da La Pi. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analogs: mung bean starch noodles (FDC ID 168869), sesame paste (FDC ID 169560), chili oil, cucumber, and carrot, weighted by typical recipe proportions. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy sesame paste fatty acid profile. Direct laboratory analysis is recommended for clinical-grade use. 2. ZERO VALUES: vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the base formulation (egg omelet is optional). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as no vitamin-D-containing ingredients are present in the standard formulation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/naturally zero for this plant-oil-based preparation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the base dish is vegan. 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL: water_g is set to -1 as moisture content for this assembled cold dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. 4. GI: GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for mung bean vermicelli and cooled starch preparations; direct measurement does not exist for this specific dish. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 13.5 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 15.2 g. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no dedicated ontology term exists for Chinese starch jelly sheet preparations as of 2024. 6. PORTIONS: The input JSON contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical 250 g entries); deduplicated to one canonical serving of 250 g. 7. DIETARY FLAGS: is_vegan and is_vegetarian set to true for the standard base formulation; egg omelet is an optional garnish that would change these flags if included.
